questions.question: What does the loopback interface (127.0.0.1) do when you send a packet to it?
options: Sends the packet to your default gateway
Broadcasts the packet to all hosts on the local network
Routes the packet back to the same machine without touching physical hardware
Drops the packet silently
questions.explanation: The loopback interface (lo) is a virtual software interface. Packets sent to 127.0.0.1 are handed back to the kernel immediately — they never reach a NIC, a cable, or any external network. This makes it ideal for testing: you can run a server and client on the same machine and capture their traffic.

questions.question: Why does packet capture require root or special privileges on Linux?
options: Because network hardware is expensive and Linux protects it
Because raw sockets can read every packet on a wire, including other users' traffic
Because tcpdump is a commercial tool that requires a license
Because the kernel limits network speed for non-root processes
questions.explanation: A raw socket receives a copy of every frame that passes through an interface — not just the frames addressed to your process. On a shared network, this means a malicious user could read unencrypted passwords, emails, or any other data. Linux therefore requires the CAP_NET_RAW capability (or root) to open a raw socket.

questions.question: After running `sudo usermod -aG wireshark $USER`, your user still cannot capture packets without sudo. What is the most likely cause?
options: Wireshark was not installed correctly
The wireshark group does not exist on this system
You have not logged out and back in since the group was added
The loopback interface does not support promiscuous mode
questions.explanation: Linux group membership is loaded when a session starts. Running `usermod` adds you to the group in /etc/group, but your current shell session still has the old group list. You must log out and log back in (or run `newgrp wireshark`) for the kernel to recognize your new group membership.

questions.question: Which tcpdump flags would you use to capture exactly 20 packets on the loopback interface without resolving hostnames?
options: tcpdump -i lo -r 20
tcpdump -i lo -n -c 20
tcpdump --interface=loopback --count=20
tcpdump -lo -no-resolve -stop 20
questions.explanation: The `-i lo` flag selects the loopback interface, `-n` disables hostname resolution (so you see IP addresses instead of names, which is faster and less ambiguous), and `-c 20` tells tcpdump to exit after capturing 20 packets. These three flags together are the standard starting point for any loopback capture.

questions.question: You run `ncat -l 9999` in one terminal and `echo 'test' | ncat 127.0.0.1 9999` in another. The listener receives 'test' and exits. What layer of the network stack did this exercise demonstrate?
options: Layer 1 (Physical) — the voltage signals on the loopback wire
Layer 2 (Data Link) — the MAC address resolution for 127.0.0.1
Layer 4 (Transport) — a TCP connection was established to carry the data
Layer 7 (Application) — the HTTP protocol was used to transfer the string
questions.explanation: ncat (netcat) opens a TCP connection by default. TCP is a Layer 4 (Transport layer) protocol. The listener on port 9999 accepted a TCP connection from the client, received the 'test' string as a TCP stream, and printed it. No application-layer protocol (like HTTP) was involved — just raw TCP over the loopback address.
